# Reflection — Read4ever

> **Note:** This feedback is AI-generated and experimental. It's meant to surface useful observations, not to grade you. Take what resonates, ignore what doesn't. Your own sense of what you learned matters more than anything here.

## Scope & Idea Clarity

**What went well:** The user definition is exceptionally precise — *"self-taught developers and students who learn from open web resources but struggle to actually finish material, maintain context across sessions, and track what they've covered... good at collecting links, bad at completing them."* That's a behavioral profile, not a demographic. It tells you exactly what the app has to do differently from Readwise or Notion. Most scope docs name who the user is; this one named their failure mode. The competitive landscape section (Readwise, Matter, Notion, PathCurator) is similarly sharp — each entry explains what's missing from that tool, not just what it does.

**What to try next time:** Several cuts in the cuts table share the same stated rationale: "library too small early on." That's one argument covering search, filter/sort, and other features that are actually quite different from each other. Give each cut its own specific rationale. The more precise the cut argument, the easier it is to know *when* to bring a feature back — and the clearer it becomes when a cut was about scope vs. when it was about genuine irrelevance.

## Requirements Thinking

**What went well:** The two-mode import dialog — simple (checkboxes only) vs. advanced (reorder, rename, description) — was introduced unprompted during the PRD conversation. It's a clean example of a requirement that traces directly to user behavior: a developer importing a quick reference guide doesn't want to rename anything; a developer building a curriculum does. That's requirements thinking, not feature listing. The Resume behavior decision ("last chapter opened" over "first incomplete chapter") is another strong moment — pushed back with a clear reading-flow rationale and stuck to it.

**What to try next time:** The `/prd` deepening rounds were skipped — moved straight to document generation. For most of the PRD that worked fine. The one place it cost something: the tag system. Tags-as-metadata landed as a Tracks replacement, but the filtering story ("no filter UI in MVP") was left open-ended and persisted as ambiguity into spec and checklist. One deepening round focused on the tag use case — who looks at tags, when, and how — would have sharpened whether tags were decorative or functional before the build had to decide.

## Technical Decisions

**What went well:** The `flutter_inappwebview` decision was made for the right reason, and the right question surfaced it — *"how do system apps do it with text selection?"* That question led directly to the native ContextMenu approach, which was architecturally cleaner than a custom overlay. The three-layer JS bridge (`js_bridge.dart` for WebView interop only, `highlight_service.dart` for domain logic, external `.js` asset files for scripts) is genuinely modular — debuggable, independently version-controllable, and survivable through a WebView package swap without touching domain logic. That structure reflects the explicit "flexible, modular, secure, scalable" constraint applied during `/spec`.

**What to try next time:** The pattern during `/spec` was: drive design decisions confidently (light-first, teal, DM Sans), defer on package-level decisions. That worked here. On a longer project, aim to own the package-level decisions more actively — understand *why* `flutter_inappwebview` over `webview_flutter`: the bidirectional JS channel model, the `evaluateJavascript` return value support, the ContextMenu API. Knowing the reasons protects you when a package releases a breaking change or a bug surfaces in production.

## Plan vs. Reality

**What went well:** 14 of 15 checklist items shipped. More importantly, the hard items shipped correctly. The XPath highlight system, the `shouldOverrideUrlLoading` redirect loop, the `onLoadStop`/error-page interaction — all required diagnosis and targeted fixes, not rewrites. The process notes show each issue was caught, reasoned through, and resolved. That's what a detailed spec makes possible: you can tell when behavior diverges from intent, because the intent is written down.

**What to try next time:** The build added two features not in the spec — in-WebView link handling (temp mode banner, "Add / Dismiss" flow) and `reader_highlight_sheet.dart`. Both were good additions. But they're undocumented. They exist in the code, not in the spec. Next time, take 5 minutes when you add something mid-build to write it into the spec: what it does, why it exists, what the intended behavior is. The person debugging that code in 6 months — probably you — will want to know the *why*, not just find code that appeared between commits.

## How You Worked

**What went well:** You arrived with strong opinions and acted on them throughout. Eliminated Tracks in favor of tags on your own initiative. Pushed back on Resume behavior with a clear rationale. Asked for more scenarios on the highlighting verification step — that single request produced a more thorough test surface for the riskiest item in the build (8 scenarios, covering DOM-spanning selections, cross-chapter bleed-through, note editing, scroll-to-highlight). For a first run through SDD, that's the right posture: the methodology is doing its job when you're using it to push back, not just to follow.

**What to try next time:** The `/checklist` conversation was the lowest-engagement phase. You accepted the proposed sequencing without modification — even though you clearly had the instincts for it (immediately identified reader/highlighting as the riskiest pieces). Sequencing a build is a real skill. Try being more opinionated about it: propose a different split, push back on the order, reason out loud about dependencies. Even when the proposed sequence is right, the act of arguing for it builds the mental model you need when something goes wrong mid-build.

## Your Goals

You came in with two goals: master SDD methodology end-to-end, and build something that feels intentionally crafted rather than generated.

On the first: you ran the full cycle — scope, PRD, spec, checklist, build, iterate, reflect. The planning artifacts actually drove the code. And the observation you made in the reflection conversation — "I'd want a spec amendment hook mid-build, because ideas come when you have a working demo" — tells me the methodology landed. You're not just done with it; you're already thinking about how to improve it for the next run.

On the second: the design system, the DM Sans + teal + NotebookLM-adjacent direction, the polished bottom sheets, the zero-duration chapter transitions, the native ContextMenu highlights — those were all deliberate choices, not defaults. That's what "non-slop" means in practice. The app looks and behaves like a tool someone thought about, because someone did.

## Reflection

**What surprised you most?** — "Even though spec-driven development is slow, the results quality is unmatched."

That tension is worth holding onto. The slowness is real — SDD is front-loaded. But vibe-coding has the inverse curve: fast at the start, expensive later (debugging, rewriting, "why does this work this way?"). SDD is slow at the start and fast later. Most of the build steps went in cleanly because the spec had already resolved the hard decisions. The instinct to skip planning when you're in a hurry is exactly when the planning matters most.

## Milestone Completion

- /prd: complete
- /spec: complete
- /checklist: complete
- /build: 14/15 checklist items completed (Devpost submission pending)
- /iterate: 3 iteration cycles (I1, I2, I3)
